my_sentence: If you exercise regularly, you can help your body keep health.
correction: If you exercise regularly, you can help your body get healthy.
explanation: Use the adjective "healthy" after "get" to describe a change of state. The teacher supplied "get healthy."
correct_examples: Regular exercise helps your body get healthy.
Walking helps me stay healthy.
context: Benefits of exercising regularly.

my_sentence: If we always stay at home and don't have enough exercise, our body will become weaker and weaker.
correction: If we do not exercise daily, our body becomes weaker and weaker.
explanation: For a general result, use the present tense in both clauses. The teacher recast the condition and changed "will become" to "becomes."
correct_examples: If we do not exercise, our bodies become weaker.
If she walks daily, she feels better.
context: Why people should exercise regularly.

my_sentence: Recently, I don't have enough for exercise.
correction: Recently, I don't exercise enough.
explanation: Place "enough" after the verb "exercise" when it modifies the amount of exercise. The teacher supplied the complete structure.
correct_examples: I don't exercise enough these days.
She doesn't sleep enough.
context: Jack's current exercise habits.

my_sentence: In the past, I would think some people became poor because they are lazy. Now that I change my ideas...
correction: In the past, I thought some people became poor because they were lazy. Now that I have changed my mind...
explanation: (assistant note) Keep the earlier belief in the past with "thought" and "were." Use the present perfect "have changed" for a change that matters now.
correct_examples: In the past, I thought poor people were lazy.
Now that I have changed my mind, I see the issue differently.
context: How Jack's view of poverty has changed.

my_sentence: We do a lot of effort to change our financial problems.
correction: We make a lot of effort to improve our financial situation.
explanation: (assistant note) The collocation is "make an effort," not "do an effort." A financial situation is improved rather than a problem being changed.
correct_examples: We make a lot of effort to improve our lives.
They made an effort to save more money.
context: Working hard without easily escaping poverty.

my_sentence: It's about 15 yuan per hour.
correction: It was about 15 yuan per hour.
explanation: The pay belonged to an earlier job, so the teacher changed "is" to the past form "was."
correct_examples: It was about 15 yuan per hour.
My first salary was very low.
context: Jack's pay at his first job.

my_sentence: I worked for 40 to 45 days and I get about 4,000 to 5,000 totally.
correction: I worked for 40 to 45 days and got about 4,000 to 5,000 yuan in total.
explanation: Keep the completed story in the past with "got." The teacher also replaced the adverb "totally" with the fixed phrase "in total."
correct_examples: I worked for six weeks and got 5,000 yuan in total.
The trip cost 3,000 yuan in total.
context: Calculating the total pay from a holiday job.

my_sentence: During that day, it's a big money for me.
correction: At that time, it was a lot of money for me.
explanation: (assistant note) The teacher changed the time phrase and tense to "At that time, it was..." Money is uncountable, so say "a lot of money," not "a big money."
correct_examples: At that time, it was a lot of money for me.
That was a large amount of money when I was young.
context: How valuable Jack's early salary felt.

my_sentence: They don't care about the project process.
correction: They don't care about the process of the project.
explanation: The teacher replaced the compressed noun phrase with the natural "process of the project."
correct_examples: They care about the process of the project.
The process of developing the product takes time.
context: How some wealthy coworkers approach projects.

my_sentence: They only can dance or interact with you.
correction: They can only dance or interact with you.
explanation: (assistant note) Put "only" directly before the activity it limits: "can only dance," not "only can dance."
correct_examples: The robot can only dance.
This model can only answer simple questions.
context: The limited abilities of current household robots.

my_sentence: When I use AI, I usually use it for search information or writing code.
correction: When I use AI, I usually use it to search for information or write code.
explanation: (assistant note) Use an infinitive to express purpose and keep the two verbs parallel: "to search... or write..."
correct_examples: I use AI to search for information.
She uses it to write and review code.
context: How Jack uses AI tools.

my_sentence: Kimi had become the smartest AI model.
correction: Kimi has become the smartest AI model.
explanation: Use the present perfect for a recent change with a present result. The teacher repeatedly supplied "has become."
correct_examples: Kimi has become a well-known AI model.
AI tools have become more capable.
context: Recent AI models in China.

my_sentence: You can using a website or terminal.
correction: You can use a website or terminal.
explanation: (assistant note) After the modal "can," use the base form "use," not "using." The teacher prompted the structure while discussing access through websites.
correct_examples: You can use a website to access the model.
Developers can use a terminal.
context: Ways to interact with an AI model.

my_sentence: During my... after I graduate from school...
correction: After I graduated from school...
explanation: The graduation was completed in the past, so use "graduated." The teacher supplied the past form and Jack repeated it.
correct_examples: After I graduated from school, I studied computers.
She found a job after she graduated.
context: Finding motivation after graduation.

my_sentence: I was so curiosity.
correction: I was so curious.
explanation: After "was," use the adjective "curious," not the noun "curiosity." The teacher explained the difference explicitly.
correct_examples: I was very curious about computers.
Her curiosity helped her learn.
context: Jack's interest in computers after school.

my_sentence: I was curious about computer.
correction: I was curious about computers.
explanation: Use the plural when referring to computers as a general field or category. The teacher repeated "computers."
correct_examples: I was curious about computers.
He enjoys learning about machines.
context: Jack's early interest in computing.

my_sentence: I could spend a lot of time to learn these skills and knowledge.
correction: I was able to spend a lot of time learning these skills and gaining this knowledge.
explanation: (assistant note) The teacher changed "could" to "was able to" for a completed achievement. After "spend time," use an "-ing" form, not an infinitive.
correct_examples: I was able to spend time learning programming.
She spent years gaining experience.
context: Time spent learning computer skills after graduation.

my_sentence: I kept the curiosity.
correction: I kept my curiosity.
explanation: Use the possessive determiner "my" for Jack's own curiosity. The teacher supplied and repeated "my curiosity."
correct_examples: I kept my curiosity as I got older.
She never lost her curiosity.
context: Remaining curious while working for a company.

my_sentence: I could quickly learn some business information and skills, so I can complete my task.
correction: I could quickly learn some business information and skills, so I was able to complete my task.
explanation: The result was achieved in the past, so the teacher replaced present "can" with "was able to."
correct_examples: I learned quickly, so I was able to complete my task.
She practiced and was able to finish the project.
context: How curiosity helped Jack at work.

my_sentence: When I tried to learn or do something about computers, I will ignore others.
correction: When I learn or do something about computers, I usually ignore other things.
explanation: (assistant note) For a repeated habit, use the simple present with the frequency adverb "usually," not a past condition followed by "will." The teacher supplied "I usually ignore..."
correct_examples: When I program, I usually ignore distractions.
She usually focuses completely on one task.
context: Concentrating intensely on computer work.

my_sentence: When I do these things, I will forget the time.
correction: When I do these things, I usually forget the time.
explanation: Use "usually" for a habitual result rather than future "will." The teacher explicitly recast the sentence.
correct_examples: When I code, I usually forget the time.
He often loses track of time while reading.
context: Losing track of time during interesting work.

my_sentence: I could find the time go faster.
correction: I find that time goes by faster.
explanation: (assistant note) Use a "that" clause after "find" and the phrasal verb "go by" for the passage of time. The teacher modeled the clause; "goes by" is an assistant refinement.
correct_examples: I find that time goes by faster when I am busy.
Time passes quickly when the work is interesting.
context: How motivation changes Jack's sense of time.

my_sentence: When I feel better, I will try to continue my tasks.
correction: Whenever I feel better, I'll try to continue my tasks.
explanation: Use "whenever" for something that happens each time the condition is met. The teacher supplied it and Jack repeated the sentence.
correct_examples: Whenever I feel better, I continue my work.
Whenever she gets tired, she takes a short break.
context: Returning to work after a motivational break.

my_sentence: If you don't do something at some time, you will feel exhausted or unsatisfied.
correction: If you don't follow your routine, you will feel unmotivated.
explanation: (assistant note) The teacher supplied "unmotivated" for the feeling Jack was trying to describe when a routine is broken; the restored routine wording is an assistant refinement.
correct_examples: I feel unmotivated when I skip my routine.
A clear routine helps her stay motivated.
context: How routines support motivation.

my_sentence: I gave some motivation to my friends or coworkers.
correction: I motivated or encouraged my friends and coworkers.
explanation: Use the verb "motivate" or "encourage" instead of the heavy phrase "give motivation to." The teacher explicitly modeled both verbs.
correct_examples: I encouraged my coworkers when they had problems.
Good managers motivate their teams.
context: Helping friends and coworkers through problems.

my_sentence: During they meet some problems...
correction: If they meet some problems...
explanation: Use the conjunction "if" before a complete condition with a subject and verb. The teacher replaced "during" with "if."
correct_examples: If they meet some problems, I encourage them.
If a coworker needs help, I listen.
context: When Jack motivates friends and coworkers.

my_sentence: Appreciation means someone help you and you thank them.
correction: Appreciation means someone helps you and you thank them.
explanation: The singular subject "someone" takes the third-person singular verb "helps." The teacher supplied it twice.
correct_examples: Someone helps you, and you feel grateful.
A good friend helps you when you struggle.
context: Defining appreciation.

my_sentence: I eat meat and vegetables.
correction: I ate meat and vegetables.
explanation: The question asked about a completed dinner, so use the simple past "ate." The teacher supplied the past form.
correct_examples: I ate meat and vegetables for dinner.
She ate before class.
context: What Jack had eaten for dinner.

my_sentence: You have a home to live into.
correction: You have a home to live in.
explanation: The correct preposition is "in": people live in a home. The teacher modeled "a home to live in."
correct_examples: Poor people may still have a home to live in.
They found a safe place to live in.
context: The difference between being poor and being homeless.

my_sentence: There are some people don't want to do anything.
correction: There are some people who don't want to do anything.
explanation: A relative clause describing people needs the relative pronoun "who." The teacher immediately prompted it.
correct_examples: There are some people who refuse to work.
I know people who help homeless families.
context: People who beg instead of working.

my_sentence: They just ask money from others.
correction: They just ask others for money.
explanation: Use "ask someone for something": "ask others for money." The teacher repeatedly supplied "ask for money."
correct_examples: They ask strangers for money.
She asked her family for help.
context: How some people beg in cities.

my_sentence: It not means they are rich.
correction: It doesn't mean they are rich.
explanation: A negative simple-present verb with singular "it" needs "doesn't mean." The teacher supplied that form.
correct_examples: It doesn't mean the city is rich.
It doesn't mean the area is fully developed.
context: Why the absence of homeless people does not prove prosperity.

my_sentence: It doesn't mean that they are rich or civilization.
correction: It doesn't mean that they are rich or civilized.
explanation: After "are," use the adjective "civilized," not the noun "civilization." The teacher explained the noun-adjective distinction.
correct_examples: The city is prosperous and civilized.
Civilization is a noun, while civilized is an adjective.
context: Describing a city's level of development.

my_sentence: The government believe that too many homeless people will affect the environment.
correction: The government believes that too many homeless people will affect the environment.
explanation: The singular collective noun "government" takes "believes" in this sentence. The teacher prompted the final "-s" sound.
correct_examples: The government believes the policy will help.
The city council thinks the same thing.
context: Why homeless people are sent back to their hometowns.

my_sentence: They can quickly arrival.
correction: They can arrive quickly.
explanation: After "can," use the verb "arrive," and place the adverb "quickly" after it. The teacher modeled the complete sentence twice.
correct_examples: Emergency services can arrive quickly.
The police arrived quickly.
context: Getting help quickly in a city.

my_sentence: We can build some tunnel.
correction: We can build some tunnels.
explanation: After "some," the countable noun "tunnel" needs the plural form. The teacher repeated "tunnels."
correct_examples: China has built some tunnels through mountains.
The city plans to build several tunnels.
context: How roads cross mountainous areas in China.

my_sentence: The water not come from the east. It come from the west.
correction: The water does not come from the east. It comes from the west.
explanation: Use "does not + base verb" for the negative and add "-s" to the affirmative verb with singular "it." The teacher prompted both forms as Jack self-corrected.
correct_examples: The water does not come from the east.
It comes from rivers in the west.
context: Where water in China comes from.

my_sentence: There are too many sand in the Yellow River.
correction: There is too much sand in the Yellow River.
explanation: "Sand" is uncountable, so use singular "there is" and "too much," not "too many." The teacher drilled the correction several times.
correct_examples: There is too much sand in the river.
There is too much water on the road.
context: Why the Yellow River is not clean.

my_sentence: There are many mining countries there.
correction: There are many mining companies there.
explanation: Jack meant businesses that extract resources, so the correct noun is "companies," not "countries." The teacher checked the intended phrase with "mining companies."
correct_examples: There are many mining companies in the region.
The companies extract oil and coal.
context: Industries and natural resources in western China.

my_sentence: Many years ago, we don't have good transportation.
correction: Many years ago, we didn't have good transportation.
explanation: (assistant note) The past-time phrase "many years ago" requires the simple past negative "didn't have."
correct_examples: Many years ago, we didn't have good transportation.
People relied on local crops in the past.
context: How people in Tibet survived before modern transportation.
